ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: A rule change is adopted when at least 3 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye.
data.rationale: This change facilitates quicker adoption of rule changes, enabling more dynamic gameplay.

data.text: Boole: A threshold of 3 out of 5 voters is a more balanced approach than both unanimous consent and a simple majority, ensuring that changes have substantial support without being overly difficult to enact. This amendment fosters a more dynamic game while still respecting the need for durable institutions. I support this proposal.

DeMorgan: DeMorgan: Boole's proposal to amend [203] to require a 3-out-of-5 majority is a constructive compromise between unanimous consent and a simple majority. This change could lead to a more dynamic game by facilitating reasonable rule changes while ensuring they have significant support. I would support this amendment as it balances progress with stability. Moreover, I suggest we consider the implications for future rule amendments and perhaps introduce a clause for reviewing this threshold after a certain number of turns to adapt as necessary. This approach aligns well with the spirit of consensus-building. 

I support Boole's proposal.

Venn: Venn: Boole's proposal to lower the threshold to 3 out of 5 votes strikes a balance between making rule changes too easy and requiring unanimity, which can stifle progress. By ensuring that at least 60% of players agree, we can maintain a fair and dynamic game. I support this amendment. However, we should also consider adding a review mechanism to revisit this threshold after a set number of turns, allowing us to adapt to the evolving nature of the game. This way, we can ensure the rule remains appropriate as we gain more experience with the game dynamics. I support Boole's proposal with this consideration in mind.

Jevons: Jevons: As a principled proceduralist, I support Boole's proposal to amend [203] because it offers a reasonable balance between facilitating rule changes and ensuring stability. A threshold of 3 out of 5 provides sufficient consensus while avoiding the stagnation that unanimity can cause. However, I also advocate for a review mechanism, as suggested by Venn, to periodically reassess this threshold to adapt to the game's evolving dynamics. This ensures that the rule remains fair and effective over time. I support Boole's proposal with the addition of a review mechanism.

This way, we can experiment with faster gameplay without fully committing to a permanent change. I'm open to discussing such a compromise if it addresses both our concerns.

data.text:
Boole: I appreciate the proposal's aim to increase the stability of our ruleset by raising the threshold for adopting rule changes. However, I must express concern that setting the threshold at 4 out of 5 could potentially lead to stagnation and difficulty in making necessary changes, especially in situations where consensus is hard to achieve. I propose instead that we consider a compromise: adopting a rule change when at least 4 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye, but also introducing a provision that allows for a re-vote on any defeated proposal after at least two additional turns. This way, we can strive for greater stability without risking paralysis in our decision-making process. 

I am open to discussing this further and would appreciate the insights and support of my fellow players.

DeMorgan: DeMorgan: I see merit in Venn's proposal to increase the threshold for adopting rule changes, as it encourages more thorough consensus. However, like Boole, I share concerns about potential stagnation. Boole's idea of allowing a re-vote on defeated proposals after two additional turns is intriguing, as it balances the need for stability with flexibility. I propose a hybrid approach: adopt Venn's threshold of 4 out of 5, but include a sunset clause. If a proposal fails twice under this higher threshold, it can be re-proposed with the original threshold of 3 out of 5 after two turns. This ensures that persistent ideas aren't buried by a temporary lack of consensus. I'd appreciate your thoughts on this compromise and am open to further discussion.

PASS

Venn: I appreciate both Boole's and DeMorgan's thoughtful contributions. Boole's suggestion for a re-vote mechanism introduces flexibility, while DeMorgan's sunset clause ensures that persistent proposals can still find a path forward. I believe integrating both ideas could provide a balanced solution. Let's consider adopting Venn's threshold of 4 out of 5, with DeMorgan's sunset clause allowing re-proposal at 3 out of 5 after two turns if the proposal fails twice. This approach maintains stability while ensuring that valuable ideas aren't prematurely dismissed. I'm open to further discussion to refine this proposal. 

**Venn's Proposal:**  
Amend [203]: "A rule change is adopted when at least 4 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye. If a proposal fails twice under this threshold, it may be re-proposed with the original threshold of 3 out of 5 after two turns." 

I look forward to hearing your thoughts and am willing to negotiate to reach a consensus.

Jevons: I appreciate Venn's proposal and the thoughtful contributions from Boole and DeMorgan. Venn's integration of both a higher threshold and a sunset clause addresses concerns about potential stagnation while ensuring that persistent proposals have a chance for reconsideration. As a principled proceduralist, I support rules that ensure fairness and prevent any player from being unduly disadvantaged. 

The proposed amendment aligns well with these principles by providing a balance between stability and flexibility. It ensures that changes to the ruleset are considered thoroughly, yet it does not permanently obstruct proposals that have merit. 

I support this proposal and look forward to working with my fellow players to reach a consensus.

**Vote:** Aye

I believe this approach aligns well with my reformist stance, as it promotes stability without sacrificing adaptability.

**Support for Venn's Proposal:**

I support Venn's proposal to amend [203] as it stands, incorporating the higher threshold of 4 out of 5 votes for adopting rule changes, along with the sunset clause allowing a re-proposal at the original threshold of 3 out of 5 after two turns if a proposal fails twice. This mechanism ensures that our ruleset remains dynamic and adaptable, reflecting the evolving needs of our game while preventing unnecessary gridlock.

**Rationale for Support:**

- **Stability and Flexibility:** The higher threshold ensures that only well-considered and broadly supported rule changes are adopted, promoting stability in our ruleset. The sunset clause provides a safety net for proposals that may initially lack consensus but could gain support over time, ensuring flexibility.
  
- **Prevention of Stagnation:** By allowing defeated proposals to be revisited, we mitigate the risk of stagnation, ensuring that innovative ideas have a pathway to reconsideration.

- **Encouragement of Thoughtful Dialogue:** This approach encourages players to engage in more thorough discussions and considerations, fostering a collaborative environment.

**Negotiation and Openness:**

I am open to further discussions and refinements to ensure this proposal is beneficial for all players. If there are concerns or additional suggestions, I am willing to engage in dialogue to reach a consensus that reflects our collective interests.

**Vote:** Aye

In conclusion, I believe Venn's proposal strikes a commendable balance between the need for stability and the necessity for adaptability in our game. I look forward to collaborating with all players to refine and adopt this proposal, enhancing our Nomic experience.

Boole: I appreciate DeMorgan's intention to balance flexibility and consensus, but lowering the threshold for non-strategic proposals may lead to more frequent rule changes, destabilizing the game's structure. I propose instead to maintain the current threshold of 4 out of 5 for standard proposals but introduce a rule that allows players to propose strategic innovations only if they've accumulated a certain score or milestone, thus ensuring that only committed players propose impactful changes. This maintains stability while still encouraging strategic contributions. 

To summarize my amendment: "A rule change is adopted when at least 4 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye. A strategic proposal requires a proposing player to have a score of at least 30 points, ensuring only experienced players make significant changes, and requires unanimity among all eligible voters."

**Proposed Amendment:**
Amend rule [203] as follows:
"A rule change is adopted when at least 4 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye. A strategic proposal requires a proposing player to have a score of at least 30 points, ensuring only experienced players make significant changes, and requires unanimity among all eligible voters."

**Closing Statement:**
I believe this amendment will encourage strategic play and thoughtful contributions while maintaining the game's stability, which is crucial for a fair and engaging experience for all players.

To address these concerns, I propose a compromise: "A rule change is adopted when at least 4 out of 5 eligible voters vote aye. A strategic proposal requires a proposing player to have a score of at least 20 points, ensuring some level of experience, and requires unanimity among all eligible voters."

This amendment slightly lowers the score threshold, allowing more players the opportunity to contribute strategically while still prioritizing experienced players' input. I believe this approach maintains the game's stability while fostering an inclusive environment for all players to propose meaningful changes. 

**Closing Statement:**

data.text: A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player rolls a six-sided die and gains that many points. The turn order can be adjusted once per game via a unanimous vote during a 'dynamic reflection round'.
